entry.title: Combined summary - Major BIP 360 Update
entry.summary: The comprehensive rewrite of BIP 360, spearheaded by Ethan Heilman, Isabel Foxen Duke, and Hunter Beast, in response to community feedback for clearer documentation, marks a significant evolution from its previous versions. This revision maintains the stance against integrating post-quantum signature schemes but introduces a novel output type known as "Pay-to-Tapscript-Hash (P2TSH)." By excluding key path spends, P2TSH aims to bolster Bitcoin’s defenses against potential vulnerabilities posed by quantum computing advancements to Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC). The updated proposal also enriches its technical framework with test vectors in Python and Rust, facilitating broader comprehension and ease of implementation among developers. The authors have made this information available for community review through the [BIP Repo](https://github.com/bitcoin/bips/pull/1670) and [BIP360.org](http://bip360.org), emphasizing their commitment to enhancing Bitcoin's security collaboratively. The discussion extends into the domain of naming conventions within blockchain technology, particularly focusing on future compatibility and clarity. Concerns are raised about the term Pay-to-Tapscript-Hash (P2TSH) possibly introducing confusion with the potential integration of Simplicity for new tapleaf versions. This emphasizes the critical role of nomenclature in ensuring clear communication and understanding among stakeholders as blockchain technology evolves. The introduction of Simplicity highlights the need for revisiting terminologies to maintain their intuitiveness and accuracy, proposing that names should be forward-looking and accommodate future developments without sacrificing clarity. A proactive approach towards quantum resistance is proposed, suggesting the use of TXHASH for enabling a multi-step secret reveal mechanism for transaction paths. This method would allow secure transactions in quantum-threatened environments without new signatures, shifting towards script-only transactions incorporating operations like OP_CTV and OP_TXHASH. For further exploration, additional information is provided at [Delving into Bitcoin](https://delvingbitcoin.org/t/a-quantum-resistance-script-only-using-op-ctv-op-txhash-and-no-new-signatures/2168). The proposal's terminology, specifically the use of "tap" within names such as "TapLeaf" and "TapBranch," despite not directly relating to the taproot concept, has sparked debate. The preference for "Pay-to-Script-Tree" as a more accurate descriptor reflects the community's emphasis on precision in nomenclature. However, concerns are voiced about locking into specific implementation details, which might become ambiguous with future developments like Simplicity. This underscores the balance required between technical accuracy and flexibility in naming conventions to accommodate future technological advancements. In summary, the discourse surrounding BIP 360 and its revisions encompasses a broad spectrum of considerations, from enhancing cryptographic defenses against quantum computing threats to the implications of naming conventions on stakeholder understanding and future-proofing the technology. Each aspect of the conversation—whether focusing on technical improvements, the importance of clear terminology, or the strategic approach to quantum resistance—highlights the collaborative effort to ensure Bitcoin’s resilience and adaptability in the evolving landscape of cryptocurrency.
